Traders who correctly predicted the shutdown would end on the 12th lost their bets due to the website administrator's delayed update. The market for Zelensky wearing a suit sparked concerns about conflicts of interest. The contract asked Ukrainian President Zelensky whether he would wear a suit on a particular occasion—a seemingly insignificant question—yet attracted over $200 million in bets. When Zelensky appeared at the NATO summit in the suit described by the BBC, the New York Post, and other media outlets, the market initially answered "yes." However, Universal Market Access (UMA Protocol) token holders disputed the result, ultimately changing it to "no." In this article, I explore how the clever combination of Large Language Models (LLMs) and cryptography can help us create large-scale prediction market solutions that are difficult to manipulate, while maintaining accuracy, complete transparency, and credible neutrality. This isn't just a prediction market problem. Similar dilemmas plague financial markets as well. The International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) has been struggling for years to address the liquidation challenges of the credit default swap market (a type of contract that pays compensation in the event of a company's or country's debt default), and its 2024 assessment report candidly pointed out these difficulties. ISDA's determination committee, composed of major market participants, votes on whether a credit event has occurred. However, like the UMA's process, it is criticized for a lack of transparency, potential conflicts of interest, and inconsistent results. The fundamental problem remains the same: when large sums of money depend on determining whether an ambiguous situation has occurred, every resolution mechanism becomes a target for exploitation, and every ambiguity can become a potential trigger. So, what should a good resolution mechanism look like? Characteristics of a Good Solution Any viable solution needs to achieve several key characteristics simultaneously. **Capability to Resist Manipulation:** If adversaries can influence outcomes by editing Wikipedia, spreading fake news, bribing authorities, or exploiting loopholes, the market becomes a game of manipulation, not prediction. **Reasonable Accuracy:** The mechanism must make correct judgments in most cases. Absolute accuracy is impossible in a world of genuine ambiguity, but systematic errors or obvious mistakes severely damage credibility. **Prior Transparency is Crucial:** Traders need a complete understanding of how the settlement mechanism works before placing bets. Changing rules during trading violates the fundamental agreement between the platform and participants. **Credible Neutrality:** Participants need to trust that the mechanism does not favor any particular trader or outcome. This is why it's so problematic to let participants holding large numbers of UMA contracts resolve the contracts they've bet on: even if they act fairly, the appearance of a conflict of interest undermines trust. Human committees can satisfy some of these characteristics, but fall short in others—especially at large scales, struggling to effectively resist manipulation and maintain credible neutrality. Token-based voting systems like UMA also suffer from well-known whale dominance and conflicts of interest. This is where artificial intelligence (AI) comes in. The rationale for using Large Language Models (LLMs) for judgment: In the prediction market space, a proposal is gaining traction: using Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges, locking specific models and hints into the blockchain when contracts are created. The basic architecture works as follows: When a contract is created, the market maker specifies not only the resolution criteria in natural language, but also the exact LLM (identified by a timestamped model version) and the precise hints used to determine the outcome. This specification is then cryptographically written to the blockchain. At the start of a transaction, participants can view the complete settlement mechanism—they know exactly which AI model will determine the outcome, what hints it will receive, and which information sources it can access. If they don't like this trading method, they won't trade. During settlement, the committed LLM runs according to the committed hints, accesses all specified information sources, and makes a judgment. This judgment determines who will receive compensation. This approach addresses several key constraints simultaneously: LLMs are highly resistant to manipulation (though not absolutely). Unlike Wikipedia pages or small news websites, the output of an LLM model cannot be easily edited. The model's weights are determined at generation time. To manipulate the results, an adversary would either need to corrupt the information sources the model relies on or contaminate the model's training data in some way well in advance—both of which are costly and extremely risky compared to bribing oracles or tampering with maps. High accuracy. With the rapid development of inference models, capable of handling a wide variety of complex tasks, especially those that can browse the web and search for new information, LLM models should be able to accurately solve many market problems—experiments to understand their accuracy are still ongoing. Complete transparency. The entire settlement mechanism is open, transparent, and auditable before anyone places a bet. There are no rule changes during the competition, no arbitrary judgments, and no behind-the-scenes deals. You are fully aware of what you are participating in. This significantly enhances credibility and neutrality. The LLM model has no economic interest in the outcome, is not bribed, and does not hold UMA tokens. Its biases, whatever they may be, stem from the model itself, not from ad-hoc decisions by stakeholders. Of course, the LLM model's judgments also have some limitations, which I will outline and discuss below: Models can also make mistakes. For example, LLM might misinterpret news reports, fabricate facts, or apply judgment criteria inconsistently. However, as long as traders understand which model they are using, they can account for its shortcomings. If a model has a particular bias in handling ambiguous cases, experienced traders will adjust it. The model doesn't need to be perfect, but it must be predictable. Manipulation isn't impossible, it's just much more difficult. If the prompt specifies a particular news source, an attacker could attempt to insert reports into those sources. This attack is costly for large media organizations but could be effective for smaller ones—essentially another form of map-tampering. The design of the prompt is crucial: a parsing mechanism that relies on diverse, redundant information sources is more robust than one that relies on a single point of failure. In theory, poisoning attacks are possible. An adversary with sufficient resources could attempt to influence the training data of an LLM model, thereby affecting its future judgments. But this requires action long before the contract is created, with uncertain returns and high costs—far more difficult than bribing committee members. The proliferation of LLM models can cause coordination problems. Liquidity becomes fragmented if different market participants choose different LLMs and trading tips. Traders struggle to compare contracts or aggregate information across markets. Standardization is important, but allowing the market to discover which LLM and trading tip combinations work best is equally crucial. The right approach might be a balance: allow experimentation, but also establish mechanisms that allow the market to gradually and consistently adopt well-tested default settings over time. How can developers adopt these strategies? In short, AI-based solutions essentially replace previous problems (human bias, conflicts of interest, information asymmetry) with a different set of problems (model limitations, engineering challenges, information source vulnerabilities), which may be easier to solve. So how do we move forward? Platforms should: Establish a track record by testing LLM mechanisms on low-risk contracts. Which models perform best? Which hint structures are most robust? What failure modes occur in practice? Standardization. As best practices emerge, the industry should work towards developing standardized LLM-hint combinations as the default option. This is not to hinder innovation, but rather to help concentrate liquidity in easily understood markets. Build transparency tools, such as interfaces that allow traders to view the full settlement mechanism (including models, hints, and information sources) before trading. Settlement mechanisms should not be hidden in inconspicuous details. Continuous governance. Even with AI judgment, humans still need to make meta-level decisions: which models to trust, how to handle situations where models give clearly incorrect answers, and when to update default settings. The goal is not to completely exclude humans, but to guide them from ad-hoc, case-by-case judgments to systemic rule-making. Prediction markets possess immense potential to help us understand the complexities of the world. However, this potential depends on trust, which in turn depends on fair contract resolution mechanisms. We have already witnessed the consequences of failed resolution mechanisms: chaos, anger, and the exodus of traders. I have personally witnessed people completely abandon prediction markets after feeling cheated because their betting results seemed to contradict their initial intentions—they vowed never to use their previously favored platforms again. This undoubtedly misses a golden opportunity to unleash the potential of prediction markets and expand their wider applications. LLM models are not perfect. But when combined with cryptography, they can achieve transparency, neutrality, and resistance to the manipulation that plagues human-based systems. This may be exactly what we need now that prediction markets are expanding far faster than governance mechanisms.
